answer:
Iqbal's "Larkiyan Parh Rahi Hain Angrezi" (Bang-e-Dara Part 3, ch. 27) adopts a sharply satirical tone to critique the blind imitation of Western education, particularly the notion that girls learning English represents the nation's path to salvation ("falāh"). The poem mocks this as a misguided "drama," portraying the rejection of Eastern customs in favor of Western styles as potentially disastrous, with the final lines building suspense around an ominous outcome.[^1]

In contrast, "Bachay ki Dua" (Bang-e-Dara Part 1, ch. 16) employs a devout, aspirational tone in the voice of a child praying for a life illuminated by true knowledge. Education here is evoked positively as a "candle" drawing the soul like a moth, integrated into broader pleas for moral service, homeland pride, and divine guidance away from wrong paths—emphasizing spiritual and ethical growth over cultural mimicry.[^2]

While both poems touch on early education's role in personal and national destiny, "Larkiyan" warns against Westernized superficiality with irony, whereas "Bachay ki Dua" idealizes knowledge as a pious, transformative force.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [ch. 27, ¶1](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/27-larkiyan-parh-rahi-hain-angrezi/translation/#p-289582).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [ch. 16](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/), esp. [¶4](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/#p-01b332) and [¶7](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/16-bachay-ki-dua/translation/#p-39abc8).
